The function `GetPowerSettings` should return error when the channel is invalid. The problem is that the function `GetPowerSettings` may need to query the RCP via Spinel protocol to get the result. If we already know the channel is invalid, we can avoid calling `GetPowerSettings` to save time. On the other hand, set the channel to `Radio::kChannelMax + 1` is part of the algorithm, we didn't really want to get the power settings of the channel `Radio::kChannelMax + 1`.

```python

        print(b"".join(stream))
```
Or writing to file:
```python
for layer in downloaded_manifest.layers:
    try:
        with client.download_blob(repository_name, layer.digest) as stream:
            with open("layer_name", "wb") as layer_file:
                for data in stream:
                    layer_file.write(data)
    except ValueError:
        # Downloaded digest value did not match. Deleting file
        os.remove("layer_file")
```

This will raise a `KeyError` if the `media` or `subs_trial_media` items do not exist on the `mdata` dict when trying to access their `url_ull` item. Both `media` and `subs_trial_media` are set to optional in the validation schema, so it's not guaranteed that `mdata["media"]` and `mdata["subs_trial_media"]` exist. `media` is the main problem here if you're using `subs_trial_media` as a fallback, unless `media` is always included in the API response. That makes flagging it as optional questionable though.

The whole plugin is in a rather bad shape and could need a proper cleanup/rewrite. For example, it queries the site's API every time for each metadata lookup (stream title and author) in addition to the regular stream URL lookup. The validation schemas are outdated and their return values are being read everywhere in an insecure way, as mentioned above. In addition to that, it uses outdated HTTPSession methods and the overall code style of how error messages are logged is not ideal either. This could all be done in a much simpler/better way.

That's all unrelated to this PR though, so please ignore what I just said.

What needs to be fixed however is how the `url_ull` items are read from the `mdata["media"]` and `mdata["subs_trial_media"]` dictionaries from the validation schema return value.

I was wondering thinking about this a lot because `len(td_error)` never seemed to fit the sequence lengths. Even if I took into account `max_seq_len` because of padding. The problem in RNNSAC is: It uses SAC's train_batch_size of 256. While padding the sequence to prepare it for feeding it into an RNN, we increase the size (to 264 in this example). This blows up our sequences and we end up having an incomplete last sequence to train on because, before learning, we cut the batch to 256. Therefore the last sequence would always be incomplete. In our Policies, we cut loaded batches if they are too long. For example this snippet is from our current TorchPolicyV2: ``` if device_batch_size >= len(self._loaded_batches[0][0]): batch = self._loaded_batches[0][0] else: batch = self._loaded_batches[0][0][offset : offset + device_batch_size] ``` So if you were to learn on a batch that has sequences, but is not padded (for whatever reason), and is larger than the device_batch_size, the produced td_error would be smaller than the number of sequences. Therefore, the discarded sequences would not produce a TD error and so `len(td_error)!=len(batch.SEQ_LENS)`.

by taking a union over all the transitive deps of all buildenvs you risk missing nodes that *should* be included in the graph, even if buildenvs are filtered.
let me describe with an example:
node A1 uses buildenv B1
node B1 depends on node C1
node A2 uses buildenv B2
node A2 depends on node C1

with this graph, C1 will be filtered out (because it's in the closure of B1), but it's wrong, because C1 is not contained in the closure of B2, so it will be built when building A2.

This allows for the same tight control over dependency versions, while
- making minor & patch upgrades (which are expected to be non-breaking) much easier;
- reducing the noise of changes in `package.json`, which allows people to use the Git file history tools much better.

There is also an underlying physical question, which requires to understand how experiments are done [ this will become a different Issue ]  : 

In my [comment](https://github.com/radis/radis/pull/106#issuecomment-615469626) I explained why the unit changes when you apply an instrumental slit function (to account for the spectrometer limited spectral resolution), if this slit function was normalized at the maximum instead of per area [ note that 99% of the people normalize at the area, we're dealing with a very particular case here, used only when calculating emission spectra to have an idea of the spectral flux]. This applied well to emission spectra.

Here, units == "1" refers to a transmittance spectra (or emissivity, but a similar reasoning applies): 

A theoretical transmittance spectrum is the ratio of an intensity I and a background I0. In practice, no-one ever measures a transmission spectrum directly. Your instrument collects an intensity I first, a background intensity I0 next, divides both and returns a transmittance spectrum I/I0. So your spectral resolution loss applies to I and I0. Your real transmittance spectrum is : `Filter(I) / Filter(I0)` instead of `Filter(I/I0)` as we are doing here. 
In practice the background is quite flat, so we have `Filter(I0)` ~ `I0` , so we end up with `Filter(I)/Filter(I0) ~ Filter(I)/I0 = Filter(I/I0)` , so applying the spectral filter to the theoretical transmittance spectrum directly is eventually a very good approximation.  But notice that  `Filter(I0)` ~ `I0`  assumed the filtering did not change the units! 

Therefore: 

- applying a slit not normalized by area to a transmittance spectrum is meaningless.  (people experienced in absorption spectroscopy like @dcmvdbekerom  will probably confirm!).

> > Regarding the global COUNTERS variable... I'm not sure that's the best approach.
> It could be read/updated not only by web requests, but by cronjobs also or even by one-off Dyno commands.
> 
> This is what the `COUNTERS_LOCK` is for:  It ensures that each thread in a process has exclusive accesses the COUNTERS.

Indeed, it ensures multiple threads from the **same** process don't step on each other's toes.
But I'm more concerned in multi process scenarios.

If we have 2 separate processes A & B, they'll both have their own COUNTERS object *(initially empty)*.
Even if they both start at the same time & lazy initialize COUNTERS from the database in the same way (such that both COUNTERS are initially the same), that doesn't guarantee the ids generated in A & B won't be duplicates; COUNTERS acts as a cache system at process level, built from scratch; A & B don't know each other's COUNTERS. When A & B will try to set these duplicated ids in the database, there are chances the client code will through errors.

In other words, A will think that "Hey! I can use the ids from this X chunk." B will consider that as well, for the very same X chunk. But when they try to sync that to the central database, one of them will fail.

> > The component Validation does not know enough information to make this decision for CompareTableView.

> I don't follow what you mean. Is there a situation where the `validated` props are not updated in `withValidation` when the relevant query strings for the compare view are changed?

Yes, the `validated` props aren't updated on time. While debugging, this is the flow we discovered:
1. We've changed the `newRevision` param.
2. `CompareTableView.componentDidUpdate()` kicks in & reaches `this.props.location.search !== prevProps.location.search`, which evaluates to true *initialy*. But the `validated` props aren't up to date *yet*. Thus, `CompareTableView` made a redundant backend request.
3. Then `validated` props get updated, causing `CompareTableView.componentDidUpdate()` to kick in again. *But*, `this.props.location.search !== prevProps.location.search` no longer evaluates to true; `location` from new props & previous props are now the same.

It's good that you are reusing the RunspacePool so we don't hit the cost of creating a new one but we are still sending 2 extra messages that we don't technically need to here, creating the pipeline, then starting to receive the pipeline output. Instead we can avoid all this and just expand the path as part of single command we are already running. The way you can do this is something like the following

```python
ps = PowerShell(pool)

# Inject the raw src/dest from the client as a variable so we don't need to escape any chars
ps.add_cmdlet('Set-Variable). \
    add_parameter('Name', 'Path'). \
    add_parameter('Value', src). \
    add_statement()
    # add_statement() is a way of splitting commands from each other. Think of it as a newline or `;` in PowerShell scripts.

if expand_variables:
    # Because `$Path` was set above we need just to expand the string based on that variable and set it back to itself
    ps.add_script('$Path = [System.Environment]::ExpandEnvironmentVariables($Path)').add_statement()

# This is the command/script that we normally run in copy/fetch
ps.add_script(command)
ps.invoke()
```

Unfortunately this does mean we need to change https://github.com/jborean93/pypsrp/blob/master/pypsrp/pwsh_scripts/copy.ps1#L105 and https://github.com/jborean93/pypsrp/blob/master/pypsrp/pwsh_scripts/fetch.ps1#L10 to use `$Path` and change those scripts to rename any existing `$path` vars to something else to avoid any collisions.

@RehanSD  it looks like you've introduced `_impure` to accommodate applying axis-wide `sample_func`, which returns both a full-1-axis pandas dataframe and a list of quantiles for the first column of that dataframe. `_impure` creates a very different alternate API for all the axis-wide functions in the partition manager layer. With `_impure=True`, the per-partition function can map from a pandas dataframe to any iterable object, and the entire function maps from lists of valid pandas dataframe partitions (i.e., each partition's `_data_` is a pandas dataframe) to lists of (possibly invalid) partitions (e.g. `PandasOnRayDataFramePartition`) of anything (you are creating a row of partitions each wrapping `np.array` of quantiles instead of pandas dataframes). I think this behavior is likely to confuse users of `map_axis_partitions`, and it's tricky to use even if you want it.

Would it work to instead crate row partitions with a single element by deploying an axis-wide identity function with `num_splits=1`? We could also create a pandas dataframe with a single row to represent the quantiles, so we always have valid partitions in the end.

Regarding the easy fix, I do think there are much more elegant and globally minimal ways of solving this matching problem. Because of the thresholding, my method will never give a "false" match (as defined by the threshold criterion) between environments, but there are edge cases where it could fail to find the match, for the reasons Vyas was saying. What I could have done originally, for example, was implement a solution to the [assignment problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assignment_problem) like the [Hungarian algorithm](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment.html#scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment). One could then check if each matched vector pair that minimizes the cost function, the sum of distances between all matched vector pairs, satisfies the threshold criterion. I have no idea about the timing of this type of solution but that would be more ideal than what I implemented, probably.

Please let me know if I understand correctly: you made a new class that reimplements what `EventWindower` was meant to do, but with the following differences: (1) it uses a new internal function (`_supercrop_starts`) instead of `mne.events_from_annotations` to identify events, (2) it uses 'crop'/'supercrop' language instead of 'windows' in the variable names and docstring, (3) it sets the default behaviour to `drop_samples=False` (instead of requiring the user to set this explicitly) and (4) it doesn't inherit from the `BaseWindower` class.

So, a couple of questions/points:

1. Before I forget, I think docstrings should start with a one-line short description on the same line as the first `"""`: see https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#documentation-strings
2. Do you see this new Windower as a replacement for `EventWindower` or is this meant as something more specific to the crop/supercrop case? If it is meant to replace `EventWindower`, why did you chose to go this route instead? Specifically, why did you chose to replace `mne.events_from_annotations`? Personally, I think the closer we stay to the MNE way of doing things, the simpler it will be to maintain the package.
3. If it is indeed meant to replace `EventWindower`, do you think we should keep the crop/supercrop-specific language? My worry is that since this class is central to the package, and these concepts are relatively niche, they might make the understanding of the library more difficult. As far as I understand, `supercrop` could just be replaced by `window` here as there is no subdivision into crops happening at this level.
4. I understand that you guys' use case is to extract a window that covers the last "missed" samples in each event. I still believe that this default behaviour can be problematic in some cases (as the opposite behaviour can be problematic in others). I liked our previous solution to this - what do you think?
5. I see there is commented code indicating that this class would also handle the use case for `FixedLengthWindower`. Are you planning on merging the two?
Thank you 👍

The problem with this solution is that when you call `request.body()`, that function [uses `request.stream()` to get the body](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/20973f2ed31f4fe7faca988f8590fe3eaf70541e/starlette/requests.py#L194-L195). Because you haven't reset the state around streams, if the user then calls `request.stream()` they will get an error.

I think if you set `request._stream_consumed = False` that will make this work.

Alternatively, you could just set `request._body` and not touch `_receive` at all. As far as I can tell, everything will work, as `request._body` [short-circuits streaming](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/20973f2ed31f4fe7faca988f8590fe3eaf70541e/starlette/requests.py#L169-L172). That removes the need for `set_body` entirely, unless I'm missing something.

- All extensions are activated (their promises pending).
- The layout restorer starts fetching its data.

- The widget manager registers a placeholder with the rendermime registry, and resolves its registry
- Extensions that supply widgets register with the registry
- The dynamic widget extension starts fetching remote scripts.
- The `app.restored` promise resolves.
- The settings registry starts fetching schemas to update settings of other extensions
- The layout restorer starts restoring trackers (e.g. notebooks)
- Widgets are instantiated
- Settings resolve for the dynamic widget extension, or its fetch results come back, and it now registers a widget type, too late.

```
in_idf = isin([True], startswith(depcom, departements_idf))
```

Je pense que ce n'est pas correct. `isin` va renvoyer un vecteur de la même taille que son premier argument, donc un seul élément dans la formulation ci-dessus, même si le vecteur `depcom` a une taille différente de 1.

Là encore il vaut mieux utiliser une opération Numpy (vectorisée) sur les chaines, à la place de l'opération correspondante en Python; en fait ce qu'on veut c'est les 2 premiers caractères de `depcom`, qui correspondent au département. C'est `numpy.char.ljust`.

```
>>> a = np.array(["93451","75001","45001"])
>>> np.isin(np.char.ljust(a,2),["93","75"])
array([ True,  True, False])
```

Donc
```
in_idf = np.isin(np.char.ljust(depcom,2), departements_idf)
```

Tout ça me fait penser au passage à https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/673 - on a eu tendance à sous-estimer l'importance pour les utilisateurs de comprendre le calcul vectoriel en général et Numpy en particulier.

Pour cette raison d'ailleurs je suis plus favorable à l'import en bloc `import numpy as np` (ou même comme recommandé par Matti `import numpy`) de préférence à l'import sélectif `from numpy import (isin, char, …)` de façon à mettre en évidence les portions du code qui appellent les primitives Numpy.

Made a change to use GH Actions to run Rust integration tests but there are some caveats:
- The GH Actions now can only run on `ubuntu-latest` but not Windows. Reason is I couldn't find a way to switch Windows Docker to use Linux Containers (unlike AppVeyor).
- Therefore, we still need AppVeyor to run these tests in Windows, which is defined in `appvyeor-windows-build-rust.yml`
- We still run the Rust integration tests in Canary (defined in appveyor-ubuntu.yml and appveyor-windows.yml) to keep the completeness of tests in Canary
- The result is - we only remove running Rust tests in the Appveyor make pr check. But the benefit is significant - we avoid adding an extra 22minutes of time to the Appveyor make pr check.
- Downside - it is a bit confusing as tests are spread across in different CI scripts. I'm a bit worried about the possibility of us missing some tests by having none of the CI scripts to pick up these tests.

> In addition, it warns about `__declspec(dllexport)`: "_If you rebuild the DLL with new exports, you also have to rebuild the applications because the decorated names for exported C++ functions might change if you use a different version of the compiler to rebuild it_".

But the same situation would also happen with def files. If a new compiler version changes the name decorating rules, then the def files won't be correct anymore, and we'll have to maintain 2 files for the old and the new version, which in my opinion is worse than using `__declspec(dllexport)`. Thankfully, both the Itanium and Microsoft C++ ABI are now fairly stable, so we don't have to worry too much about symbol names over e.g. CRT mismatch.

> "This is very convenient if you are designing a DLL for use by many applications because you can add new functionality and also ensure that it continues to work correctly with the applications that already rely on it"

This is really only useful when we're exporting using ordinals, in which case it might be helpful to maintain compatibility with old DLLs. But considering that the library would be tied to the finished product, and also the fact that we don't provide prebuilt binaries, the chance of a DLL swap in a finished product is slim. Besides, we're exporting by names anyway, so it doesn't really matter. I haven't seen a case when `__declspec(dllexport)` actually fails.

I thought about the fact that we're modifying `subextras`, but it's created as a new dictionary in `create_extras`, so modifying it is not a problem.  It's pretty clearly outside the intended usage of the `Lazy` object to call `construct()` multiple times, and I'd rather not program so defensively that I worry about misuse of `Lazy` in here.

Which one should take precedence / should we give a warning is a separate issue.  I think you're misunderstanding what comes from where in this method.  Things that you specify in a config file will end up in `popped_params`, not `subextras` or `kwargs`.  These will take precedence, yes.

The things in `subextras` are things that came from `**kwargs` higher up in the object hierarchy.  This is like, `Model.from_params(params=params["model"], vocab=vocab)`.  `vocab` here ends up in `subextras` as an available argument if a particular constructor wants it, but is ignored if the constructor doesn't need it (this is how, e.g., the `Embedding` gets the `Vocabulary` - the `TextFieldEmbedder` passes it on from the model using this mechanism).

The things in `**kwargs` in this context are things that are passed directly to `lazy_object.construct()`.  You shouldn't need to pass things along that are already passed along in `subextras`, but if you do on accident, you would get a crash because the same key showed up twice.  I'm pretty sure I ran into this when I was doing `learning_rate_scheduler.construct(num_epochs=num_epochs)`.  `num_epochs` in that setting comes from `**kwargs`.  But it also was in `subextras`, because it gets passed along as an available argument to subclasses if they request it, using the mechanism I described above.  So, I should probably have just removed the `num_epochs` argument to the `construct()` method that I called, but that actually makes the code a little less clear, I think, and it's easy to handle that case here, anyway.

If I had *also* directly specified `num_epochs` in the parameters for the learning rate scheduler, I'm not sure how that's handled.  It might do the right thing, and it might crash.  I'll add a case for that tomorrow in my test and make sure that it does the right thing.

One thing we should document better than we currently have is the distinction between the output of `split_query()` and the output of `make_query_plan()` -- I think that would clarify the situation here. `split_query()` tells us where we cross schemas, but does not specify an execution order. `make_query_plan()` specifies an order between the subqueries, and is free to specify any valid order -- and may choose an optimized order if it has access to statistics etc.

Given that, once a query plan is made, it's always executed from the root onward. We may in the future allow different styles of executors (e.g. DFS, BFS, async + parallel across children, etc.), but this executor function is just a simple sync DFS.

I agree that the first issue is _currently_ not covered as of the latest commit; that was a rushed job on my part to push the changes out before I went away and is my mistake, I blindly followed your suggested code instead of the _intent_ behind your comment, and forgot to add the second criterion in, which would of course solve `init_guesses is None`, which I can add; thanks for spotting the missing code. 

I'm not sure I follow on 2 and 3, however: 2 is covered by the code I did add to the `elif` catch after checking for `sigma` or `FWHM` attributes, isn't it? The docstring demands that `init_guesses` be an `astropy.table.Table` so checking for `flux_0 not in init_guesses.colnames` _does_ cover option 2, surely? All I did here was use the same criterion you did, so if yours worked I can only assume mine would too...

Similarly for 3, `fitshape` is the _routine's_ attribute, not the model (`self.fitshape` vs `self.psf_model.fitshape`), and cannot _not_ be input, with the docstrings requiring `int or length-2 array-like`; indeed, allowing `fitshape=None` as an input into `BasicPSFPhotometry` et al. is on my to do list, and thus (for now, at least) `self.fitshape` is _always_ acceptable (or the code falls over on the user before this point).

All of this is actually irrelevant, and my mistake for not realising and explaining my previous generic `else` catch more fully earlier: the next [block](https://github.com/astropy/photutils/blob/b9ba45925bcbe3d269e5f52f28282a59ef24f8e2/photutils/psf/photometry.py#L265-L299) of code just below this bit in `BasicPSFPhotometry` covers both of these eventualities: the first part covers `if init_guesses is not None and flux_0 not in init_guesses.colnames` while the `else` is therefore equivalent to `if init_guesses is None`. These two parts of the code block handle these issues, but simply require `aperture_radius` to be set, so we can just _always_ set `aperture_radius` if it isn't provided (and warn the user, of course), because code _already exists_ to account for these two issues.


I therefore actually would prefer, for the sake of clarity and code simplicity, to revert this minor change and go back to a generic `else` catch (with some comments -- either in the code or to the user -- to inform of why this is fine) after failing to find `sigma` or `FWHM` attributes. I will make these tests to ensure that these criteria behave as expected, of course, but with the code block already in place to handle the exceptions I think there shouldn't be any issues. If the fitting routine cannot find a `sigma` or `FWHM` attribute in `self.psf_model` then it falls back on an `aperture_radius` which is (hopefully) sensible (and gives the user to the warning to input their own, better one if it isn't), and uses that in the two cases we're worrying about, just as it would have done if the user had supplied their own `aperture_radius` anyway.

This actually then ties nicely into #945, as with that reverting to a generic `else` catch the code block for testing for the need of a fallback `aperture_radius` is _now_ in agreement between `BasicPSFPhotometry` and `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` and could be split off into a private helper function as originally desired; it is the next code block in `BasicPSFPhotometry` that handles the additional edge case interactions with `init_guesses` which the `_do_photometry` part of `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` doesn't have to deal with, since it always has to do aperture photometry for its runs beyond the first and so is forced to have an `aperture_radius` (handling its setup equivalent to `init_guesses is None` all the time).

I actually think the problems with `BaseHTTPMiddleware` are terminal: it's not fixable. 

I also disagree about what the problem is: I don't think Starlette is offering too many low-level tools but not encouraging developers to use them _enough_. For example of this, the _other_ middleware shipped by Starlette works fine, and those all take the `scope, receive, send` args. Here, Starlette's layers-of-an-onion design where everything is an ASGI app that wraps another ASGI app (until you get to the center where a `Request` object is created) is both simple and makes a lot of sense in my opinion: even after all this time I can still hold almost the whole Starlette codebase in my head. If other libraries (such as the starlette-prometheus one I linked above) wrote their Middleware using this style, they'd probably be fine. We've written lots of our own Middleware using these patterns at work and our background tasks do run in the background: we don't have this issue.

I actually think we run into problems with the leaky abstractions in Async programming in Python in general when we start to work with the following:

- Streaming response bodies
- Background tasks

My suspicion is that to fix these problems you would like have to spin up channels and _separate_ the processing of requests -> responses, requests -> streaming responses, and background. This is probably where the refactoring effort should be placed. A Starlette app should spin up a dedicate channel for background tasks and sending responses and then the `Response` class should route things to the proper channel.

The real problem in my opinion is _ever_ thinking about a "complete" `Request`, `Response`, or `Background` when everything should be treated as streaming by. You can only `map` or `filter` over these things or send work to other channels. (My last preference here would be to leverage the kinds of tools that `trio` provides, so you get much improved channels, nested scoping, and cancellation over standard library async, but that would probably be a disruptive change for Starlette users.)

Again, I offer my opinions humbly as someone who has a lot of respect for the Starlette codebase, someone who sees this project as a very nice implementation of the Python ASGI spec and someone who's built a couple dozen projects at work using it.

NOTE:
Before, the `owner` of actor will always cache miss, so that the `ray.get_actor(actor_name)` will always get actor from GCS, and then call `AddNewActorHandle` to ensure the rcorrectness of actor's reference.

This PR: the `owner` of actor can still get named actor from `cached_actor_name_to_ids_`, so we need add this logic to avoid bad actor's reference.

Why the `owner` of actor will always cache miss?
1. `CoreWorker::CreateActor` --> `ActorManager::AddNewActorHandle` --> `ActorManager::AddNewActorHandle(empty_actor_name)` --> `ActorManager::AddActorHandle` will insert a new actor handler to `actor_handles_`, and the empty_actor_name will never be put into `cached_actor_name_to_ids_`.
 2. Then, `ray.get_actor(actor_name)` will get named actor from GCS, the chain is `CoreWorker::GetNamedActorHandle` --> `ActorManager::GetNamedActorHandle` --> `Actors().SyncGetByName` --> `ActorManager::AddNewActorHandle`. The subcripiton will not be invoked anymore due to there is already an actor handle inside `actor_handles_` generated in step1.

So, no mater how many times the `ray.get_actor(actor_name)` is invoked, the named actor will never be put into `cached_actor_name_to_ids_`.

What if `da` is an integer or string or some other invalid value?  I think it would be confusing for the user if they were trying to create a MultiTag and they got an error about DataArray creation with invalid data.  We should catch this issue as early as possible, preferably in the `create_multi_tag()` method in the `Block` class.

Also, I think this might not be the best location for the convenience functionality to be in general.  Having it here makes it possible to create DataArrays by assigning values to `mtag.positions`.  While this might sound useful, it also makes it more likely that a user tries to do something like:
```python
mtag.positions = [10, 11]
# ... later
mtag.positions = [10, 11, 12]
# or
mtag.positions += [13]
```
which will lead to all sorts of errors: Duplicate names for the DataArray creation, attempting to append a list to a DataArray, etc.

I think the convenience should only be in the `block.create_multi_tag()` method and nowhere else, and it should be documented clearly in the arguments in the docstring.  The type check for the `positions` argument in `create_multi_tag` should check for `DataArray` or any kind of `sequence`.

1) Should probably include the [StackName](https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/2.6.0/troposphere/__init__.py#L518) psuedoparam as a prefix here before the resource id (`CheckAuth` in this case) to keep it narrowed to its namespace

3) For normal lambda function operation, it needs to start with `/aws/lambda/FUNCTIONAME` and then the lambda@edge executions need `/aws/lambda/REGION.FUNCTIONAME` -- to really go all out here (and we're already in for a penny ....) I think we'll want to provide the resource of `/aws/lambda/StackName-ResourceName-*` & `/aws/lambda/*.StackName-ResourceName-*`
4) Not a separate change from what was noted in point 3 ^, but just to highlight something implied by it: the CreateLogGroup & CreateLogStream permissions apply to log-group ARN (e.g. `/aws/lambda/StackName-FunctionResource-*`), and the PutLogEvents permissions should apply to the log-stream ARNs (`/aws/lambda/StackName-FunctionResource-*:log-stream:*`). The flow is:

```
if no data key:
    data key, EDK = generate()
    new materials = materials(with data key)
else:
    EDK = encrypt(data key)

new materials = materials with data key(with EDK)
```

I went with that flow so that we could single-source the "with EDK" bit, but because of that, if we want to retain an available reference to the materials in the request we have a choice: we can either copy the request materials first:

```
copied materials = copy(request materials)

if no data key:
    data key, EDK = generate()
    copied materials = copied materials(with data key)
else:
    EDK = encrypt(data key)

copied materials = copied materials(with EDK)
```

or we can balance a null value

```
new materials = None

if no data key:
    data key, EDK = generate()
    new materials = request materials(with data key)
else:
    EDK = encrypt(data key)

if new materials is None:
    new materials = request materials(with EDK)
else:
    new materials = new materials(with EDK)
```

The copy flow seems cleaner to me.

Currently, we have a job `packages` within the workflow `release` that executes the `python setup.py sdist`.
The `publish-pypi` download the result of the `packages` job which is a list of distribution package.
AFAIU, the `python setup.py sdist` wasn't needed because it was already performed in another job within the `release` workflow.

In our case (within the `release` workflow), we can remove the `python setup.py sdist` step from the `publish-pypi` job and rely on the `packages` job to do it or rely on the `publish-pypi` job to do it and remove the `packages` job.
The first option avoid duplicate CI code since those steps are already present in the `packages` workflow definition.

> To me, this problem seems similar to some of the problems surrounding static initialization in C++ (e.g. https://isocpp.org/wiki/faq/ctors#static-init-order). Both to avoid the static initialization order fiasco, and to make initialization lazy, it's often best to defer initialization until the thing being initialized is used.
> 
> The best practice for this, in both C++ and Python, seems to be simply to perform the initialization in some function, so that the initialization only occurs when the function that needs it calls it. This is simple and easy to understand, and is self-documenting.
> 
> Building custom machinery around this to me sounds like trying to create a C++ wrapper around arbitrary types that avoids calling the constructor until it's actually used. Isn't it simpler just to do what the language supports directly?

Thanks for sharing this. I don't know there's actually called Construct On First Use Idiom. I use it all the time but don't know its name. Expanding this topic, there's also destruct order issues on static objects. I've seen some in our codebase too. I manually fix with dependency injection. So I think these 2 are twin problems. 

Back to the point, the initialization is done by `import`. So the initialization dependency topology is constructed by different imports. And `import` implementation is using this `Construct On First Use Idiom` by checking the singleton in `sys.modules` (similar to static Foo* bar = new Foo(); return &*bar). Besides that, you can think `import` as a **factory function** instead of the **constructor**. There's no risk to cause `static initialization fiasco`. 

The main concern here is still the maintenance and I fully understand the concern. Thanks for your knowledge sharing, that's really helpful for me.

`debug.hpp` is a core-internal private header file, so technically should not be exposed/seen by the platform code. And `OT_ASSERT` macro is intended for the OT core itself.

Some OT configs actually configure  the implementation of `OT_ASSERT()` to use the `otPlatAssertFail()` provided by platform, so we rely on platform layer to provide the assert functionality for core (won't make sense to then allow platform to use the core definition).

Keeping it separate also allows  project integrators be selective about where to enable/disable asserts (e.g., disable assert in OT core but keep it enabled at platform layer or in some specific driver).

We can consider treating assert functionality similar to `common/code_utils.hpp` which is for core only, but then we added similar helper macros in `example/utils/code_utils.h` for platform layers to use.

I am in need of a second opinion on the new implementation of `collection.find_in_box`

Given the deprecation of the `WITHIN_RECTANGLE` AQL function, we are now opting to use the `GEO_CONTAINS` method in combination with the `GEO_POLYGON` method: https://www.arangodb.com/docs/stable/aql/functions-geo.html#within_rectangle

The `GEO_CONTAINS` method takes two parameters: the polygon, and the coordinates of the document.

Given that the attribute names representing the coordinates of the document  can vary on a user-basis, we cannot assume that `doc.longitude` and `doc.latitude` will always exist.

In order to prevent any breaking changes to `collection.find_in_box`, we rely on the existing `index` parameter to fetch the Geo Index's `fields` values to use as the document's coordinate attributes.

Therefore, we assume that the Geo Index's `fields` is an array containing either 2 values at most. Otherwise, a `ValueError` is raised.

For example, if you do not need to access `len()` (or e.g index access), `Iterable` will do it and is more flexible. For instance, it allows you to also pass in an iterator (`yield "foo"`). 

But if you need to get the length or need to access elements at a specific index, then one has to tighten the type to something like `Sequence` because `Iterable` simply can't give you that (e.g. it is perfectly valid for an `Iterable` to be infinite, so not having an answer to `len()` at all).

I think moving all the global methods would make sense, or at least the combinators. From a developer perspective, having them be context-less introduces the potential to wonder about if it's possible to pass in multiple outputs with different contexts and what underlying context the combinator then uses. When, in reality, I can't think of any scenario where one would do something like that and would expect all outputs to share the same context as the combinator. Moving the combinators to the context-level would make it fairly unambiguous that the combinator operates purely within that context. One could make a similar argument for the other methods (e.g. does `ToSecret` have some context that's different from the underlying `Output`'s?) so I would argue for moving them all to hang off context as opposed to leaving them at the top-level.

Agree, something specific in this case would be better than forcing CoAP Blockwise (which doesn't fit our use case very well).

On the 2nd idea of waiting for the CoAP ACK: this Ack, in response to a TMF Answer, is in fact (or should  be) a "piggybacked response" which contains the Ack with the 2.04 response. The latter (response code) is for sure something that the CoAP module ought to expose to the next higher layer. Because it is really an application-level concept.  So at CoAP module level it would make perfect sense to expose this response code and use a callback or so to get its value back to the layer using CoAP.  

Exposing the ACK itself in this way would make less sense, I think, this is really a CoAP-internal layer used for the reliability.  At least normally the application code doesn't need to be informed of all the details of retransmissions or notifying the application that the request was received - which the Ack is about.  But because of the piggybacked communication pattern the Ack and the REsponse tend to get named interchangably, but in principle these are different concepts.

One added benefit of the diagnostic layer getting back the CoAP response code from the entity requesting the data is that it can be used for control of the remainder of Answers:  for example if the requesting entity uses 2.xx success response then the Router can continue sending more Answers. If the entity uses an error code (4.xx / 5.xx) then this may signal the Router to stop sending further Answers.

The `pwd` command uses the `${PWD}` by default, otherwise it asks the system.

^ Direct copypaste for `Bash 5.0.17(1)-release`:
```
$ help pwd
pwd: pwd [-LP]
    Print the name of the current working directory.

    Options:
      -L        print the value of $PWD if it names the current working
                directory
      -P        print the physical directory, without any symbolic links

    By default, `pwd' behaves as if `-L' were specified.

    Exit Status:
    Returns 0 unless an invalid option is given or the current directory
    cannot be read.
```
^ Here's the system-provided one's manpage (`man pwd`):
```
NAME
       pwd - print name of current/working directory

SYNOPSIS
       pwd [OPTION]...

DESCRIPTION
       Print the full filename of the current working directory.

       -L, --logical
              use PWD from environment, even if it contains symlinks

       -P, --physical
              avoid all symlinks

       --help display this help and exit

       --version
              output version information and exit

       If no option is specified, -P is assumed.

       NOTE: your shell may have its own version of pwd, which usually supersedes the version described here.  Please refer to your shell's documentation for details about the options it supports.

AUTHOR
       Written by Jim Meyering.

REPORTING BUGS
       GNU coreutils online help: <https://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/>
       Report pwd translation bugs to <https://translationproject.org/team/>

COPYRIGHT
       Copyright © 2018 Free Software Foundation, Inc.  License GPLv3+: GNU GPL version 3 or later <https://gnu.org/licenses/gpl.html>.
       This is free software: you are free to change and redistribute it.  There is NO WARRANTY, to the extent permitted by law.

SEE ALSO
       getcwd(3)

       Full documentation at: <https://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/pwd>
       or available locally via: info '(coreutils) pwd invocation'
```

So yeah, the `pwd` command can fail to find the path if your read+execute access to the folder was revoked just prior to the command or made unavailable by connection loss like in the case of NFS.

Which means it's just better to use the `pwd` command (or, if you wanna force the use of the system-provided one: `\pwd` or `command pwd`), and better yet, save the response like you're already doing, but also add a check in case it fails (I'm using `_current_folder` as an example):
```bash
# shellcheck disable=SC2155  ##We already check the emptiness.
[ ! "${_current_folder}" ] && {
    echo 'Failed to find the current working folder. Quitting.'

    exit 1
}
```

> Should this explode? If you were using a lockfile, it would imply that your constraints file was not being used for that usecase, and instead would likely be a hand-written thing used to pin down your transitive deps: overwriting it here would be strange.

It doesn't seem strange to me:

1. You use a constraints file and create a lock on some day.
2. Later, you learn one requirement you over-zealously had pinned in the constraints file has a security issue and by upgrading or downgrading you can avoid the security issue and still maintain compatibility.

So you use `-p` to over-ride the constraint. Top actually pass in a new, edited constraints file means re-running the whole lock and you don't want to do that.

What is strange is that the constraints recorded in the lock file are not edited by the over-ride. I'll fix that.

I think ultimately we make these changes here to camel case and everything else that we can leave snake cased should probably be snake cased. My reasoning is that the method arguments to python functions are expected to be snake cased, by convention. They are just variables up until we construct this `TypedDict`. Once we start building the `TypedDict`, this is the one translation step that basically says "Ok, we passed everything around using python conventions but now we need to speak to a service that does not use python conventions". The way I see it behaving is these `TypedDict` request param classes are the translation layer between python and provider and I think ultimately this is the only place we need the explicit change to camelCase.

every call to `gmfs_calculation_methods` returns a function (`calculate_gmfs`). In these two cases the function looks the same (except that one has L@Z+mu and the other one sig@Z+mu). But I will add for example something along those lines:

`@gmfs_calculation_methods.add('MMI')
def gmfs_calculation_methods_basic(kind, shakemap, imts):
    # create diag matrix with std values
    std = numpy.array(shakemap['std']['MMI'])
    sig = numpy.diag(std.flatten())  # shape (M*N, M*N)

    # mu has unit (pctg), sig has unit ln(pctg)
    def calculate_gmfs(Z, mu):
        return sig @ Z + mu

    return calculate_gmfs`

cls.register_jvm_tool(register, 'scalafix-tool-classpath', classpath=[])

The `try_recv()` method will eventually fail due to a timeout.  The call to `send_message(...)` does two things:  (1) it instantiates a `NetworkReplyHandle` for the caller to wait on, and (2) it sets up a corresponding `ReceiverNotify` struct in the underlying `Connection`'s `ConnectionOutbox` that will get used to awaken the `NetworkReplyHandle` when either a reply arrives or the request times out.

The timeouts are handled in the main loop in `p2p.rs`, in `dispatch_network()`.  In this method, it will call each ongoing `Conversation`'s `clear_timeouts()` method, which will inspect the `ttl` of each `ReceiverReply` and if it exceeds the current time, it will destroy it.  This causes the `SyncSender` channel in the `ReceiverReply` to be dropped, which will cause the next call to `try_recv()` in the `NetworkReplyHandle` to fail with `Err(net_error::ConnectionBroken)`.

```python
import warnings

class Source(...):
    ...

    # TODO: remove this in next major version
    def source_transactions(self, **params):
        warnings.warn(
            "source_transactions is deprecated, use list_source_transactions instead",
             DeprecationWarning
        )
        return self.request(...)
```

We could also add a Python docstring to display the deprecation in most IDEs:

```python
    def source_transactions(self, **params):
        '''source_transactions is deprecated, use list_source_transactions instead

        ...
```

I was testing on CPython 3.8. When I saw that the second version actually got a bit slower, I remembered that starting in Python 3.7 there's the new bytecode `LOAD_METHOD` and `CALL_METHOD` that optimize method calling and avoid creating temporary method objects. So this form of optimization can actually turn out to be a pessimazation on those versions!

Given:
```python
In [1]: class C:
   ...:     def meth(self):
   ...:         pass
   ...:     def call_meth_direct(self):
   ...:         self.meth()
   ...:     def call_meth_indirect(self):
   ...:         m = self.meth
   ...:         m()
   …:
```

The direct call results in 10 bytecodes:
```pycon
In [3]: dis.dis(C.call_meth_direct)
  5           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
              2 LOAD_METHOD              0 (meth)
              4 CALL_METHOD              0
              6 POP_TOP
              8 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             10 RETURN_VALUE
```

While the version that stores it in a variable first misses the optimization and is bigger:
```pycon
In [5]: dis.dis(C.call_meth_indirect)
  7           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
              2 LOAD_ATTR                0 (meth)
              4 STORE_FAST               1 (m)

  8           6 LOAD_FAST                1 (m)
              8 CALL_FUNCTION            0
             10 POP_TOP
             12 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             14 RETURN_VALUE
```

So what about Python 2.7, where the optimization isn't present? I get 14.7ms as-is, and between 14.3ms and 15.9ms with the modified version on two subsequent runs (IPython on 2.7 doesn't report standard deviation, just the best time).

Args that apply to all worker manager types need to go before the worker manager type and args that are specific to this worker manager type need to go after the argument. 

The problem with this setup is that if we ever add another worker manager type in the future, we'd need to add a separate entry in `docker-compose.yml` for it with the correct argument structure for it + we'd need to add all its arguments as arguments to `codalab_service.py` as well.

I don't know if there's an ideal solution here. Maybe making `--worker-manager-args` a single freeform string argument to `codalab_service` and passing just that in `docker-compose.yml` to the worker manager? Or getting rid of the subcommand structure for the `worker-manager` types and adding every argument for every type of worker manager into the same argument namespace and passing in everything that's set. 

I also feel like this decision can be  postponed to when a second worker manager type is about to be added.

That file will contain two variables:

from spyder_terminal.config import CONF_DEFAULTS, CONF_VERSION

Keep in mind that there's a big difference between:

1. Using `set` to modify the shell's default behavior and sourcing a script.
2. Ignoring that script's exit code.

The former causes the script to execute in your modified environment changing the default behavior of the shell interpreting the script while the latter treats the script like a black box and only worries about the exit code the script returns to the caller.

By using `/opt/rh/rh-python36/enable` directly, I think we're reaching into the internals of the package therefore I don't think we should rely on having consistent behavior. Instead, I think Red Hat wants you to use `scl` (or in very rare cases `scl_source`) to interface with these scripts.

As a test, I added the following to the top of `/opt/rh/rh-python36/enable` and both `scl` and `scl_source` still work:
```
try_something
if [ "$?" -ne 0 ]; then
  do_fallback
fi
```
If they did the equivalent of `set -e; . /opt/rh/rh-python36/enable` it would not.

Since the official scripts do not do this, I do not think it is worth doing it ourselves as I think it makes certbot-auto too fragile to changes I suspect the SCL maintainers view as internal.

With that said, I think checking the exit code of `. /opt/rh/rh-python36/enable` is a good idea. Both `scl` and `scl_source` does do this so I'd prefer this code looks something like:
```
set +e
if ! . /opt/rh/rh-python36/enable; then
  error 'Unable to enable rh-python36!'
  exit 1
fi
set -e
```

> But the behavior of batch request specifically mention that the server must return an array with all results, including error payloads when needed

This is also valid for single calls (https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#response_object):

> When a rpc call is made, the Server MUST reply with a Response, except for in the case of Notifications. The Response is expressed as a single JSON Object, with the following members:

With these two guidelines (single/batch) in mind, a 200 status code should always be returned along with a valid JSON response. And avoid this response customization (in `views.py`):

```py

    return handler.result_error(e, HttpResponseForbidden)

This almost fixes the problem, but with one annoying subtlety - f0 values in the input can be given as "negative" frequency values - in this case, the difference should be computed over all the reference frames, using the absolute value of the frequency given. It's not very explicit in the definition, but it's meant to be a way to measure pitch/chroma accuracy independent of voicing mistakes:
> Raw chroma accuracy: the fraction of voiced frames in ref_cent for which est_cent provides a correct frequency values (within cent_tolerance cents), ignoring octave errors

Consider the case:
```
ref_t = [1,2,3]
est_t = [1,2,3]
ref_f = [440, 440, 440]
est_f = [-220,-220,-220]
```
The Raw Chroma Accuracy should be 100%, even though the estimate voicing is `[0,0,0]`. 

So instead, I propose the following fix that solves the bug but doesn't break the negative frequency use case.
```
matching_voicing = ref_voicing * (est_cent > 0)
frame_correct = (np.abs(cent_diff - octave)[matching_voicing] < cent_tolerance)
```
In short, this only count frames as "correct" if the estimate is non-zero (where zero indicates an unvoiced frame where no "best guess" frequency is given) AND within the tolerance.

In fact, this bug exists for raw_pitch_accuracy too... but the "problem" case never comes up because we almost never report frequencies below 40 Hz, so these cases always fall outside of the tolerance threshold. For good measure though, we should make the same fix for raw_pitch_accuracy. (cc @craffel )

The general issue potentially affects anyone who sets the `sampling_rate` explicitly in `BIDSLayout.get_collections` or `load_variables`. A sophisticated user could certainly know enough about their dataset to think "I'll use a sampling rate of 50, because that's high enough to account for my short-duration events, but still manageable computationally". Thereafter, I think that user could reasonably expect that if they call `ToDense` or `Convolve` without explicitly specifying a different sampling rate, the resulting variable will have an effective sampling rate of 50.

I think making decisions for the user internally is fine if there's an explicit argument in the transformation that implies as much. I.e., if `ToDense` has default `sampling_rate='auto'`, then the docstring can just explain that we will act intelligently unless a numeric value is passed, and the result may not equal what's currently set in the collection. That seems fine. The issue is that in this case, there's no provision to specify the sampling rate in `Convolve`. I was against doing that because I don't think the spec should have that argument, but I guess we can make it a pybids-only argument, if only for the sake of making it clear to a sophisticated user what's going on.

These callables are problematic here, due to the way webgrid instantiates columns (see `Column.new_instance`). When you set up your grid:
```
class MyGrid(Grid):
    Column('My Column', my_column_expr, ...)
```
Your column's `__init__` is run at declaration time, and you get a column instance. When the grid is used at runtime, we create runtime instances of these columns with some rather unfortunate magic, which copies attributes from the original "definition" instance to the new one. So, the callables here are being evaluated only once, the first time the grid module is imported, rather than each time the grid is used.

What we need to do is, pass through these attributes as-is. `render_in` will either be a tuple or a callable. Then I see two options:
- update all usage of `render_in` and `visible` to respect the callable. This seems inefficient.
- put a new method on the grid to resolve these lambdas, and call it in `new_instance` following the attribute assignments

I cannot see though how choice of the box can influence the configuration (structure) of a snapshot of a system if one has periodic boundaries. Consider a snapshot of an infinite system of particles periodic in all directions with a given periodicity. Now attempt to tesselate the space with a shape that obeys the periodicity. Any shape that obeys the periodicity will produce the same system configuration. See the image for an example. The key here is that Lx and Ly parameters obey the periodicity in both box cases.

Let's imagine that the `current` is `(n1, n2)` and the `picked` is `(n3, n4)`.
By publishing `(n3, n4)` to DCS we are giving them a green light for the leader race.
BUT! In the `synchronous_standby_names` they are not yet declared as synchronous, therefore we can't guaranty no data loss during failover.

Order matters a lot here:
1. First you should "remove" from DCS `current` names that are not in `picked`.
2. Update `synchronous_standby_names` by putting `picked` names there.
3. Wait for some of `picked` nodes to become really synchronous.
4. Publish into DCS `picked` nodes that are already synchronous.

Basically, the [quorum state resolver](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/pull/672/files#diff-a839e95c7271edf3b62934f9e0e61f16R18) is the attempt to implement such an algorithm.

```
import torch

def foo(img):
    t_img = torch.from_numpy(np.array(img))
    do_squeeze = False
    if t_img.ndim == 2:
        do_squeeze = True
        t_img = t_img.unsqueeze(-1)
    t_img = t_img.permute(2, 0, 1).contiguous()
    t_img = t_img.to(torch.float32)

    t_out = F.gaussian_blur(t_img, kernel_size=(3, 3), sigma=(0.5, 0.5))

    np_out = t_out.cpu().numpy()
    np_out = np.transpose(np_out, (1, 2, 0))
    if do_squeeze:
        np_out = np_out[:, :, 0]
    return PIL.Image.fromarray(np_out, mode=img.mode)
```

I looked more into this. Looks like this is an error in inferring scale_factor in ONNX Runtime.
Upsample_bicubic2d is exported to ONNX and running in ONNX Runtime if you have an input like: torch.randn(1, 2, 4, 6, requires_grad=True)
ONNX graph also looks ok. 
But the same mode fails for an input like torch.randn(0, 2, 4, 6, requires_grad=True)
The reason is that ONNX Runtime is inferring scale for across all dimensions:
    for (size_t i = 0, end = input_dims.size(); i < end; ++i) {
      scales[i] = static_cast<float>(output_dims[i]) / static_cast<float>(input_dims[i]);
    }
For a 4D input, output_dims is inferred output shape (e.g. [n, c, h*scale, w*scale]) and input_dims is actual input shape (e.g. [n, c, h, w]).
This is a bug in such edge cases where n = 0.
We will create an issue for ORT to address this.

Just to note, it might be worth updating now before or when dropping the legacy processing (and updating the actual backend code accordingly), but the user-facing messages in `ratings.py` really should avoid referring to metadata fields using to to-be-deprecated, Setuptools-specific parameter names, as opposed to either the standardized source metadata key names in PEP 621 (probably best long-term, since that's what users are or will shortly be seeing in all modern tools) or the standard names of the core metadata fields.

For reference, PEP 621 (and PDM, Hatch and the other tools that use it), Flit and Poetry all have a `urls` _key_ to map to the `Project-URLs` _field_, equivalent to the Setuptools `project_url` _parameter_ (with no explicit mapping to the legacy `Home-page` field that the Setuptools `setup.cfg`/`setup.py` `url` parameter corresponds to), so this is likely to be confusing to users of any other tool, and even Setuptools itself once users adopt the recently-implemented PEP 621 support and `setup.cfg` is eventually deprecated, 

Also, perhaps a bit pedantic, but I'd really suggest using "field" correctly and consistently. "Field" refers to the core metadata _fields_, where the fields are called `Home-page` and `Project-URLs`, and nor are these the PEP 621 _key_, which is just called `urls`. Instead, these are the Setuptools _parameters_ `url` and `project_urls`.

```py
from torchvision.prototype import features, transforms

xyxy_bounding_box = transform(bounding_box)

or

```py
from torchvision.prototype import features

)
```

to

```py
from torchvision.prototype import features

Hmm that is surprising that there's a 10-20% regression with shared_ptr. One thing is that the microbenchmarks may not be reliable on a laptop (I've seen much greater variation than 10-20% across different runs of the same code). Also, if you use `shared_ptr &` instead of `shared_ptr`, it should not be any different from this method, I think.

By the way, you can't convert a shared_ptr to a unique_ptr or vice versa. Both of them are different ways to manage memory with compile-time safety checks, compared to just calling `new` and `delete` on a raw pointer. The code you have right now is just accessing the raw pointer address that's managed by the shared_ptr. This is not necessarily bad, but we try to avoid it because the caller could then store the raw pointer somewhere else, but then the address may not be valid later on. This is different from a `shared_ptr &`; if the caller wants to store that, it has to copy it to a new `shared_ptr` so we still get memory safety in that case.

I noticed that the API callback definition is mentioning "Advertising Proxy" as the intended receiver of this callback and that what the user should do in callback (e.g. "should publish update with mDNS"). I understand that this would be the common way the SRP server would be used, but thinking more abstractly, should we try to describe SRP server callback reports. Basically SRP server should be more abstract and not aware of who/what entity is using its API (i.e. whether its an Advertising Proxy or not). 

I would suggest describing what it reports and what we expect:
- This callback is notify the user that there is some update requests for a given host (`aHost`).
- The user should then process the info (check for name conflicts, etc). 
- We expect the user then to call another server API to indicate the result of its processing of the SRP update request.
- We can then give more details as example if the user is an advertising proxy (e.g., it would publish it, etc).

I don't think we benchmarked it, more based on reasoning. Back-of-the-envelope math:

- the target types we have seem to roughly be 3-8 fields, so let's average to 5 fields/tgt.
- imagine the user operates on 500 targets
- then we would be storing 2500 extra `Address` objects, in addition to the 500 already stored on the owning `Target` objects.

An insight from the original AsyncField concept: when we use fields in rules, we want to be able to operate on that single Field instance without needing to consider the rest of the target's Fields. This improves cache keys. So those fields must have an Address instance; but when we're not using the Rules API, we can simply say `tgt[MyField]` and we can easily get the address from the original target.
Is the benefit worth the code size overhead? I don't expect such checks to be useful in production.

for your comment regarding the exception we raise in line 229, this is related to the Terraform limitations we have, at this point we could not differentiate if the customer is really linking to 2 different resources, or using some conditional expression to choose one of them. As, we do not know what to be done in this case, shall we use the 2 destination resources, or choose one of them, so, we decided to raise an exception to the customer that we do not support this case, and they can resolve this issue by running `terraform apply` command, so we can use a different linking approach where we do not have any issues.

But, for no destination resources found at all, this means that there is no destinations at all to be linked, and should not be an issue (this like if the customer does not have any layers defined for a lambda function), so we do not raise exception here. Sometimes, it can be an issue in the customer terraform project, but we decided not to raise the exception here, and leave this decision to each SAM CLI command to decide if it is an issue or not (Like the customer define a Rest API method, but does not link it to a Lambda function)

Floats can be quite buggy across browsers. Based off what I see on [Can I Use](https://caniuse.com/?search=order), you could use `order` which I think is a bit more accurate to the intent here.

With either `float` or `order`, I think there's an important note regarding accessibility in the [MDN `order` docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/order):
> Since order is only meant to affect the visual order of elements and not their logical or tab order...

> Using the order property will create a disconnect between the visual presentation of content and DOM order. This will adversely affect users experiencing low vision navigating with the aid of assistive technology such as a screen reader. If the visual (css) order is important, then screen reader users will not have access to the correct reading order.

I don't think the DOM order is all that important, but specifically the keyboard navigable ordering seems important. The [linked article](https://tink.uk/flexbox-the-keyboard-navigation-disconnect/) has some recommendations.

You are right, the test in question will work on main and fail on this PR branch, and this is because on main `publicID` is used as the identifier for the default graph in addition to the base for relative URI resolution in source documents, while in this PR branch, it is only used as the base for relative URI resolution.

With the issues that this PR tries to address, what we need to answer is:

1. How should parse work? If it should work the way it works in main, we close the issues and move on, if not, we have to change how it works, which means the behaviour will be different after changing it.
2. What should we do about the issues this tries to address?

I think the way it is working in main, i.e. using publicID as the name of the graph that triples inside the default graph is loaded into, is wrong. This is also not what the documentation of publicID suggests will happen.

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/ad56044cf3033f8d2a3c625d322f3d091d7c9322/rdflib/graph.py#L1416-L1418

Given that description, what I would expect is that the `publicID` is what relative URIs will be resolved against. And that is also what it is used for, and the only thing it should be used for, I think. And I don't see why the base for URI relative URI resolution should be the same as the graph name that default triples are being loaded into, which should not be named to begin with because as per the spec, has no name:

https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-dataset

> Exactly one default graph, being an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). The default graph does not have a name and may be empty.

Another way to think about this is, what is worse:

1. Not having the content of default graph in sources loaded into the default graph of Dataset/ConjunctiveGraph
2. Having to explicitly load something into a sub-graph if you don't want it in the default graph

I think 1 is worse.

This is the right idea, but took it a bit far.  You don't need to import everything with the `es_` and `os_` prefixes, just import them normally under the correct name. You shouldn't need to create `os_Q` and `es_Q` only to assign it to `Q` later.  You can just import it as Q:


```python
if getattr(settings, 'SEEKER_OPENSEARCH_DSL', False):
    import opensearch_dsl as dsl
    from opensearch_dsl import A, Q, Search
    from opensearch_dsl.aggs import Terms
    from opensearch_dsl.connections import connections
    from opensearch_dsl.field import Object
    from opensearch_dsl.response import Response
    from opensearch_dsl.utils import AttrList
    from opensearchpy.exceptions import AuthorizationException, NotFoundError
    from opensearchpy.helpers import bulk, scan
else:
    import elasticsearch_dsl as dsl
    from elasticsearch.exceptions import AuthorizationException, NotFoundError
    from elasticsearch.helpers import bulk, scan
    from elasticsearch_dsl import A, Q, Search
    from elasticsearch_dsl.aggs import Terms
    from elasticsearch_dsl.connections import connections
    from elasticsearch_dsl.field import Object
    from elasticsearch_dsl.response import Response
    from elasticsearch_dsl.utils import AttrList
```

Then, from the rest of the package, you can do `from seeker.dsl import Q` and it will grab either the opensearch Q or the ES Q depending on which one the site uses (for example)

Making a few notes mainly for my benefit for when I work on this refactor, and offering up an alternative `Batcher` that addresses a few of my concerns.

> `   ray.wait(blocks[i:i + num_prefetch])   # avoid itertools (principle of least power)`

The point of using itertools is to grab prefetching windows from the `BlockList` iterator without forcing creation of all blocks (e.g. with creating a list), which would break the laziness of `LazyBlockList` ([you left this very feedback](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/16853#discussion_r663203529) on an earlier version of this PR). We can do the equivalent slicing on an iterator with `itertools.islice(i, i + num_prefetch)`, but that's just leaking the itertools implementation details that the `window()` utility is attempting to hide by providing a sliding window iterator.

> `        output = []`

I'm assuming that we'd still want to use the `DelegatingArrowBlockBuilder` as the output buffer.

```python
def __iter__(self):

```


### Batcher concerns:

1. If a single block contains a lot of batches, we'll end up creating (a) more slices, and (b) larger slices. E.g., the following slices would be made for `block_size = 10` and `batch_size = 2`:
    - Current - 5 batch slices: slice(0, 2), slice(2, 4), slice(4, 6), slice(6, 8), slice(8, 10)
    - Proposed - 15 batch slices: slice(0, 2), slice(2, 10), slice(0, 2), slice(2, 8), slice(0, 2), slice(2, 6), slice(0, 2), slice(2, 4), slice(0, 2)
    - Not a big deal if these slices don't involve copies.
2. In general, the above `Batcher` seems geared towards batching across blocks, including being able to add `N` blocks before extracting batches. I believe that batching within blocks (`batch_size < block_size`) will be the more common usage, and I don't think that we'll need to add more than one block to the batcher before we'll want to try to extract and yield batches (YAGNI).
3. Making callers check if the batcher has more batches to return before calling `.next_batch()` is forcing a LBYL antipattern on the caller, when EAFP is more idiomatic Python (especially when the `StopIteration` raised by the generator is very naturally handled by Python for loops).

### Alternative batcher

Oriented towards `batch_size < block_size` rather than `batch_size > block_size`, and only needing to extract batches from one block at a time (with possible remainder from previous block(s)):

```python
batcher = Batcher(batch_size=batch_size)
for block_window in window(self._blocks, prefetch_blocks + 1):
    block_window = list(block_window)
    ray.wait(block_window, num_returns=1, fetch_local=False)
    block = ray.get(block_window[0])
    for batch in batcher.extract_batches(block):
        yield format(batch, batch_format)

if batcher.has_remainder_batch() and not drop_last:
   yield format(batcher.get_remainder_batch())


class Batcher:
    def __init__(self, batch_size):
        self.batch_size = batch_size
        self.leftover = DelegatingArrowBlockBuilder()

    def has_remainder_batch(self):
        return self.leftover.num_rows() > 0

    def extract_batches(self, block):
        batch_size = self.batch_size or block.num_rows()
        if self.has_remainder_batch():
            start = min(batch_size - self.leftover.num_rows(), block.num_rows())
            self.leftover.add_block(block.slice(0, start))
            if self.leftover.num_rows() == batch_size:
                yield self.leftover.build()
                self.leftover.clear()
        else:
            start = 0
        while start <= block.num_rows() - batch_size:
            yield block.slice(start, start + batch_size)
            start += batch_size
        if start < block.num_rows():
            self.leftover.add_block(block.slice(start, block.num_rows()))

    def get_remainder_batch(self):
        return self.leftover.build()
```

I tried to balance again the two approaches, but when I consider the development workflow, I still really think it makes things clearer.

Let's consider things like this: you cannot use os.rename, because it does not work on windows, you cannot use os.replace because it does not work on python 2. You need to use filesystem.replace.

In the case of os.rename without forbidding the method by an exception, you would get the error from the CI when code is tested I. Appveyor. For os.replace without forbidding, it would be because lint is not happy, or when executing tests in the CI on Python 2.

So two very different errors should deliver the same message: do not use them, use filesystem instead.

Here I think we simplify the development workflow by giving the same behavior (a runtime exception) with an explicit message about what should be used.

Most of the objects are documented and should be referred to via their full names. As in `package.subpackage.module.WebsocketFrame`. But if you just use `WebsocketFrame`, it's hard for Sphinx to guess what you mean. There's syntax for making sure that when you use the full reference, it'll still be rendered as the short one. It RST, it needs a tilde:
```rst
:class:`~package.subpackage.module.WebsocketFrame`
```
the above will be rendered as `WebsocketFrame` in HTML while referencing a specific documented object.
If you do
```rst
:class:`package.subpackage.module.WebsocketFrame`
```
it will be rendered as `package.subpackage.module.WebsocketFrame`.
You can also enter a custom anchor:
```rst
:class:`some thing <package.subpackage.module.WebsocketFrame>`
```
This one will be rendered as `some thing` while linking the proper target.

However, another approach to packaging Python libraries has been created, it's more modern and was [accepted as a PEP](https://peps.python.org/pep-0518/), and it's the approach that Poetry uses.

        # how mypy treats annotations that do not specify an element type.
        return args[0] if args else cast(type, Any)

    raise AssertionError(f"Unexpected type. Expected 'list' got '{typ}'")
